title: Dog Umbilical Hernia Bulging: When to See a Vet
meta_desc: Is your dog's umbilical hernia bulging? Learn why this happens, how to check for emergencies, and why a photo is vital for veterinary triage.
content_html:
What is an Umbilical Hernia?

An umbilical hernia occurs when the abdominal wall fails to close completely at the site where the umbilical cord was attached. This leaves a small hole that allows abdominal contents—usually just fat, but sometimes loops of intestines—to bulge out under the skin. It typically appears as a soft bump or protrusion near the center of your dog's belly.

Is a Bulging Hernia an Emergency?

The urgency level for a bulging umbilical hernia is generally Medium. Most umbilical hernias are 'reducible,' meaning the contents can be gently pushed back into the abdomen without pain. However, if the hernia becomes 'incarcerated' or 'strangulated,' it becomes a life-threatening emergency. If the bulge suddenly feels hard, warm, painful to the touch, or appears red or purple in color, you must seek veterinary care immediately, as this indicates that the blood supply to the trapped tissue has been cut off.

Why a Photo Helps Triage

Providing a clear, well-lit photo of the bulge is incredibly helpful for veterinary triage. A photo allows a professional to assess the size, shape, and color of the hernia from a distance. By comparing the photo to known clinical signs of strangulation, a vet can help determine if your dog needs to be seen in the next hour or if the situation can wait for a scheduled appointment.

faqs.question: Can a dog's umbilical hernia heal on its own?
faqs.answer: While very small hernias in young puppies may occasionally close as they grow, most umbilical hernias require surgical repair, which is typically performed at the same time as a spay or neuter procedure.

faqs.question: What are the signs of a strangulated hernia?
faqs.answer: Signs include vomiting, lethargy, loss of appetite, and a hernia site that is firm, painful, swollen, or discolored (red, purple, or dark blue).

faqs.question: How do I know if the hernia is reducible?
faqs.answer: A reducible hernia feels soft and 'squishy.' If you apply very gentle pressure, the contents should easily slide back into the abdominal cavity. If it feels stuck or firm, do not force it.
